items.q: Does a catalyst increase the amount of product formed?
items.correct: No — it only makes the product form faster; the final amount of product is unchanged.
items.misconception: Using a catalyst gives a higher yield as well as a faster reaction.
items.random_wrong: Catalysts slow reactions down to make them safer.
items.why: Students blend rate and yield into one idea of a 'better' reaction; a catalyst changes how fast, never how far.

items.correct: No — melting is a physical change; no new substance is formed and the change is easily reversed by cooling.
items.misconception: Melting is a chemical change because the solid turns into something that looks different.
items.random_wrong: Melting is a reaction because it needs energy to happen.
items.why: Students use appearance change as their test for reactions; the real test is whether a new substance with different properties forms.

items.q: In Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu, which species is reduced?
items.correct: The Cu²⁺ ions are reduced — they gain electrons to form copper metal, while the zinc is oxidised.
items.misconception: The zinc is reduced because it disappears as it dissolves into the solution.
items.random_wrong: The sulfate ions are reduced to sulfur.
items.why: Students judge by what they see vanish or appear instead of tracking electron transfer between the species.

items.q: Blue hydrated copper(II) sulfate turns white on heating. Adding water to the white solid:
items.correct: Turns it blue again, showing the change is reversible
items.why: It's Turns it blue again, showing the change is reversible. “Has no effect on it” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which change slows a reaction down?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Lowering the temperature. “Adding a catalyst” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A graph of gas volume against time becomes horizontal when:
items.misconception: The catalyst has been used up
items.random_wrong: The gas has turned into a liquid
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The reaction has finished. “The catalyst has been used up” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu, the zinc is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Oxidised. “Reduced” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A reaction produces 48 cm³ of gas in 60 seconds. Calculate the average rate in cm³/s.
items.why: Exact answer: 0.8 cm³/s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
